title: Java Cheatsheet - Complete Reference
description: Master Java from basics to advanced with a unified Swiggy-style food delivery example throughout

concepts.name: Your First Java Program
concepts.explanation: Every Java program starts with a class and a main method. Think of the class as a Restaurant building, and main() as the front entrance where customers (Java) enter to start their food ordering journey.
keyPoints: public = anyone can access (like a public restaurant)
static = belongs to the class itself, not specific objects
void = method doesn't return anything
main = Java's entry point - execution starts here
String[] args = command-line arguments for the program
Every Java file must have a class with same name as filename
Compilation: javac SwiggyApp.java → creates SwiggyApp.class
Execution: java SwiggyApp
Interview: Why is main static? So JVM can call it without creating object

concepts.name: Primitive Data Types
concepts.explanation: Java's building blocks for storing simple values. In our Swiggy app, we use different types to store order quantities, prices, ratings, and status flags.
keyPoints: int is default for whole numbers (4 bytes, -2B to 2B)
double is default for decimals (8 bytes, high precision)
long needs 'L' suffix: 1000000L, float needs 'f': 3.14f
boolean for yes/no flags (true/false only, not 0/1)
char uses single quotes: 'A', String uses double: "Hello"
Use underscores for readability: 1_000_000
byte/short rarely used - int is preferred for integers
Default values: int=0, double=0.0, boolean=false, char='\u0000'
Interview: What's difference between float and double? Precision (32-bit vs 64-bit)

concepts.name: Variables and Constants
concepts.explanation: Variables store data that can change (like order status), while constants store fixed values (like GST rate, platform fee).
keyPoints: Variables declared with: dataType variableName = value;
Constants use 'final' keyword - cannot be reassigned
Constant names in UPPER_SNAKE_CASE by convention
Variables use camelCase: orderTotal, customerName
Use constants for values that never change (rates, limits)
final prevents reassignment but doesn't make objects immutable
Local variables must be initialized before use
Interview: Can you change final variable? No, compilation error

concepts.name: Type Casting
concepts.explanation: Converting one data type to another. Like converting total bill (double) to rounded rupees (int), or expanding small quantity (byte) to larger count (int).
keyPoints: Implicit casting: smaller → larger type (automatic, safe)
Explicit casting: larger → smaller type (manual, may lose data)
Syntax for explicit: (targetType) value
Widening: byte→short→int→long→float→double (automatic)
Narrowing: reverse direction requires explicit cast
Casting int to double before division prevents integer division
Be careful: casting large long to int may overflow
Interview: What's difference between implicit and explicit casting?

concepts.name: Arithmetic Operators
concepts.explanation: Basic math operations for calculating order totals, discounts, delivery fees, and splitting bills.
keyPoints: + addition, - subtraction, * multiplication
/ division (int/int gives int, use double for decimals)
% modulo (remainder - useful for even/odd checks)
++ increment by 1, -- decrement by 1
Order of operations: *, /, % before +, -
Use parentheses to control order: (a + b) * c
Interview: Difference between i++ and ++i? Post vs pre-increment
i++ returns old value then increments, ++i increments then returns

concepts.name: Comparison Operators
concepts.explanation: Compare values to make decisions - check if order qualifies for free delivery, if restaurant is highly rated, if customer is eligible for discounts.
keyPoints: == checks equality, != checks inequality
> greater than, < less than
>= greater than or equal, <= less than or equal
Returns boolean (true or false)
For Strings, use .equals() not ==
Example: "Biryani".equals("Biryani") → true
Interview: Why not use == for Strings? Compares references, not content

concepts.name: Logical Operators
concepts.explanation: Combine multiple conditions - check if customer is premium AND order is above threshold, or if restaurant is open OR has late-night delivery.
keyPoints: && (AND): true only if BOTH conditions are true
|| (OR): true if AT LEAST ONE condition is true
! (NOT): inverts boolean value (true → false, false → true)
Short-circuit: && stops if first is false, || stops if first is true
Use parentheses for clarity: (a && b) || c
Interview: What is short-circuit evaluation? Stops evaluating once result is determined

concepts.name: Ternary Operator
concepts.explanation: Compact if-else in one line - quickly assign delivery fee based on order value, or set order priority based on customer type.
keyPoints: Syntax: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse
Returns a value (can assign to variable)
More concise than if-else for simple assignments
Avoid nested ternary - reduces readability
Perfect for simple true/false decisions
Both value expressions must be same type
Interview: When to use ternary? Simple conditional assignments only

concepts.name: if-else Statements
concepts.explanation: Make decisions based on conditions - apply discounts, check delivery eligibility, validate orders.
keyPoints: if executes block only if condition is true
else executes when if condition is false
else-if for multiple conditions (checked in order)
Use curly braces {} even for single statements (best practice)
Conditions must evaluate to boolean
First true condition executes, rest are skipped
Interview: What happens if no condition is true? Nothing executes (unless else exists)

concepts.name: switch Statement
concepts.explanation: Choose between multiple options based on a value - handle order status, payment methods, delivery zones.
keyPoints: switch compares value against multiple cases
break prevents fall-through to next case
default executes if no case matches (optional but recommended)
Works with: byte, short, int, char, String, enum
Cannot use: long, float, double, boolean
Cases must be compile-time constants
Interview: What happens without break? Fall-through to next case
Java 12+: switch expressions with -> (no break needed)

concepts.name: for Loop
concepts.explanation: Repeat code a specific number of times - process menu items, calculate totals, display order history.
keyPoints: Syntax: for (initialization; condition; update) { }
initialization runs once at start
condition checked before each iteration
update runs after each iteration
Use when you know number of iterations
i++ is most common update (increment by 1)
Can use i-- to count backwards
Interview: What's scope of loop variable? Inside loop only (if declared in for)

concepts.name: Enhanced for Loop (for-each)
concepts.explanation: Iterate through collections easily - process all menu items, customers, orders without index management.
keyPoints: Syntax: for (Type variable : collection) { }
Simpler than regular for loop
No index management needed
Cannot modify array elements directly
Cannot iterate backwards
Cannot access index (use regular for if needed)
Works with arrays and collections (ArrayList, etc.)
Interview: When to use for-each? When you need to process all elements sequentially

concepts.name: while Loop
concepts.explanation: Repeat while condition is true - wait for order confirmation, process queue, retry failed operations.
keyPoints: Syntax: while (condition) { }
Condition checked before each iteration
May not execute at all if condition is initially false
Use when number of iterations is unknown
Be careful: infinite loop if condition never becomes false
Update condition inside loop to avoid infinite loop
Interview: Difference between while and do-while? while checks condition first

concepts.name: do-while Loop
concepts.explanation: Execute at least once, then repeat while condition is true - show menu at least once, validate input.
keyPoints: Syntax: do { } while (condition);
Executes body at least once before checking condition
Condition checked after each iteration
Use when you need to execute at least once
Common use: input validation, menu systems
Don't forget semicolon after while(condition);
Interview: When to use do-while? When body must execute at least once

concepts.name: break and continue
concepts.explanation: Control loop flow - exit early when order found, skip cancelled orders, stop on error.
keyPoints: break: exits loop immediately
continue: skips current iteration, moves to next
break works with for, while, do-while, switch
continue works with for, while, do-while only
In nested loops, break/continue affects innermost loop only
Use labeled break/continue for outer loops (rare)
Interview: Difference between break and continue? break exits, continue skips

concepts.name: Array Basics
concepts.explanation: Fixed-size containers for storing multiple values of same type - menu items, prices, customer ratings.
keyPoints: Fixed size - cannot grow or shrink after creation
Zero-indexed: first element at index 0
Syntax: Type[] name = new Type[size];
Initialize with values: Type[] name = {val1, val2, ...};
Access: array[index], Modify: array[index] = value
Length: array.length (property, not method)
ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException if index < 0 or >= length
Default values: int=0, double=0.0, boolean=false, objects=null
Interview: Array vs ArrayList? Array fixed size, ArrayList dynamic

concepts.name: Multi-dimensional Arrays
concepts.explanation: Arrays of arrays - restaurant menu with categories, delivery zones with areas, order history by month.
keyPoints: 2D array: Type[][] name = new Type[rows][cols];
Initialize: Type[][] name = {{row1}, {row2}, ...};
Access: array[row][column]
Jagged arrays allowed (rows of different lengths)
3D arrays possible: Type[][][] name
Interview: How to find length? array.length for rows, array[i].length for columns

concepts.name: Array Utility Methods
concepts.explanation: Java provides Arrays class with utility methods for sorting, searching, comparing, and filling arrays.
keyPoints: Arrays.sort() sorts in ascending order (modifies original)
Arrays.binarySearch() requires sorted array, returns index or negative
Arrays.fill() sets all elements to specified value
Arrays.copyOf() creates new array with copied elements
Arrays.equals() compares content, not references
Arrays.toString() converts array to readable string
Time complexity: sort O(n log n), binarySearch O(log n)
Interview: How to sort in descending order? Use Collections.reverseOrder() with wrapper types

concepts.name: String Basics
concepts.explanation: Strings are immutable sequences of characters - customer names, addresses, menu items, order IDs.
concepts.code:
String name = "Rahul Kumar";

int len = name.length();  // 11
char first = name.charAt(0);  // 'R'

String firstName = name.substring(0, 5);  // "Rahul"
String upper = name.toUpperCase();  // "RAHUL KUMAR"

String orderId = "ORD" + "12345";  // Concatenation
keyPoints: Strings are immutable - cannot be changed after creation
String pool: literals stored in special memory area
Use .equals() for comparison, not ==
== compares references, .equals() compares content
charAt() throws StringIndexOutOfBoundsException if invalid index
substring(start, end): end is exclusive
Interview: Why are Strings immutable? Security, thread-safety, caching

concepts.name: String Methods
concepts.explanation: Common string operations for validation, formatting, searching in customer data and menu items.
keyPoints: trim() removes leading and trailing whitespace
isEmpty() checks if length is 0, isBlank() checks if only whitespace
contains() is case-sensitive
replace() replaces all occurrences
split() uses regex pattern
indexOf() returns -1 if not found
All methods return new String (immutability)
Interview: Difference between isEmpty() and isBlank()? isBlank checks whitespace too

concepts.name: StringBuilder
concepts.explanation: Mutable string for efficient concatenation - building order summaries, generating receipts, creating dynamic messages.
keyPoints: StringBuilder is mutable - can be modified
More efficient than String concatenation in loops
Not thread-safe (use StringBuffer for thread-safety)
Initial capacity: 16 characters
Automatically grows when needed
append() is most common operation
toString() converts to String
Interview: String vs StringBuilder? String immutable, StringBuilder mutable and efficient for concatenation

concepts.name: Classes and Objects
concepts.explanation: Classes are blueprints, objects are instances. A Restaurant class defines what all restaurants have (name, rating, menu), and each restaurant object (McDonald's, KFC) has its own specific values.
keyPoints: Class = blueprint/template, Object = actual instance
Fields store object state (data)
Methods define object behavior (actions)
Constructor initializes object (same name as class, no return type)
this keyword refers to current object
new keyword creates object and calls constructor
Each object has its own copy of instance variables
Interview: What is 'this'? Reference to current object

concepts.name: Encapsulation
concepts.explanation: Hide internal details and provide controlled access - protect customer balance, validate order quantities, ensure data integrity.
keyPoints: private = accessible only within the class
public = accessible from anywhere
Getters provide read access: getFieldName()
Setters provide write access with validation: setFieldName()
Encapsulation protects data integrity
Prevents direct access to fields
Allows changing internal implementation without affecting users
Interview: Why encapsulation? Data hiding, validation, flexibility

concepts.name: Inheritance
concepts.explanation: Child class inherits properties and methods from parent class - DeliveryPartner and RestaurantOwner both inherit from User, avoiding code duplication.
keyPoints: extends keyword establishes inheritance
Child inherits all non-private members from parent
super() calls parent constructor (must be first line)
super.method() calls parent method
protected = accessible in same package and subclasses
@Override annotation indicates method overriding
Java supports single inheritance only (one parent)
Use interfaces for multiple inheritance
Interview: Why single inheritance? Avoids diamond problem

concepts.name: Polymorphism - Method Overriding
concepts.explanation: Child class provides specific implementation of parent method - different order types calculate total differently.
keyPoints: Method overriding: child provides specific implementation
Method signature must match parent (name, parameters, return type)
@Override annotation recommended (compile-time check)
Runtime polymorphism: method called based on actual object type
Parent reference can hold child object: Order order = new PremiumOrder()
Overridden method called based on object, not reference type
Cannot override: final, static, private methods
Interview: Overriding vs Overloading? Overriding = same signature in child, Overloading = same name different parameters

concepts.name: Polymorphism - Method Overloading
concepts.explanation: Multiple methods with same name but different parameters - addItem() can add single item, multiple items, or item with quantity.
keyPoints: Method overloading: same name, different parameters
Compile-time polymorphism (decided at compile time)
Parameters must differ in: number, type, or order
Return type alone doesn't distinguish methods
Compiler chooses method based on arguments
Can overload constructors too
Improves code readability and flexibility
Interview: Can you overload main()? Yes, but JVM only calls main(String[] args)

concepts.name: Abstraction - Abstract Classes
concepts.explanation: Abstract classes provide partial implementation - Payment is abstract, specific payment methods (UPI, Card, Cash) implement the details.
keyPoints: abstract class cannot be instantiated
Can have abstract methods (no body) and concrete methods (with body)
Child must implement all abstract methods or be abstract itself
Can have constructors, fields, and concrete methods
Use when classes share common behavior but differ in implementation
0-100% abstraction possible
Can extend only one abstract class
Interview: Abstract class vs Interface? Abstract can have state and concrete methods

concepts.name: Abstraction - Interfaces
concepts.explanation: Interfaces define contracts - what a class must do, not how. Trackable interface ensures Order and DeliveryPartner can both be tracked.
keyPoints: Interface = 100% abstraction (before Java 8)
All methods public and abstract by default
All fields public, static, and final by default
Class can implement multiple interfaces
Use 'implements' keyword
Must implement all interface methods
Java 8+: can have default and static methods
Java 9+: can have private methods
Interview: When to use interface? When unrelated classes need same behavior

concepts.name: Access Modifiers
concepts.explanation: Control visibility of classes, methods, and fields - protect sensitive data, expose public API.
keyPoints: public: accessible everywhere
private: accessible only within the class
protected: accessible in same package and subclasses
default (no modifier): accessible in same package only
Use private for fields, public for methods (generally)
Encapsulation: private fields + public getters/setters
Interview: Access modifier order? public > protected > default > private

concepts.name: try-catch-finally
concepts.explanation: Handle errors gracefully without crashing - handle payment failures, invalid orders, network issues.
keyPoints: try: code that might throw exception
catch: handle specific exception type
finally: always executes (cleanup code)
Multiple catch blocks: specific to general
finally executes even if return in try/catch
Use e.getMessage() for error message
Use e.printStackTrace() for full stack trace
Interview: When does finally not execute? System.exit() or JVM crash

concepts.name: throw and throws
concepts.explanation: Manually throw exceptions or declare that method might throw - validate order data, enforce business rules.
keyPoints: throw: actually throws an exception
throws: declares method might throw exception
throw used inside method body
throws used in method signature
Checked exceptions must be handled or declared
Unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException) don't need throws
Can throw multiple exception types: throws IOException, SQLException
Interview: Checked vs Unchecked? Checked must be handled, Unchecked are runtime errors

concepts.name: Custom Exceptions
concepts.explanation: Create domain-specific exceptions - InvalidOrderException, PaymentFailedException, RestaurantClosedException.
keyPoints: Extend Exception for checked exceptions
Extend RuntimeException for unchecked exceptions
Provide meaningful error messages
Can add custom fields and methods
Use for domain-specific errors
Makes code more readable and maintainable
Interview: When to create custom exception? When built-in exceptions don't convey business meaning

concepts.name: ArrayList
concepts.explanation: Dynamic array that grows/shrinks - store menu items, orders, customers. Most commonly used collection.
keyPoints: Dynamic size - grows automatically
Backed by array internally
Initial capacity: 10, grows by 50% when full
Maintains insertion order
Allows duplicates and null
Fast random access: O(1)
Slow insertion/deletion in middle: O(n)
Not thread-safe (use Collections.synchronizedList() or CopyOnWriteArrayList)
Interview: ArrayList vs Array? ArrayList dynamic, Array fixed size

concepts.name: LinkedList
concepts.explanation: Doubly-linked list - efficient insertion/deletion at ends. Good for queue of pending orders, delivery partner assignments.
keyPoints: Doubly-linked list implementation
Efficient insertion/deletion at ends: O(1)
Slow random access: O(n)
More memory per element (stores prev/next references)
Can be used as Stack, Queue, or Deque
Maintains insertion order
Allows duplicates and null
Not thread-safe
Interview: ArrayList vs LinkedList? ArrayList for random access, LinkedList for frequent insertions/deletions

concepts.name: HashMap
concepts.explanation: Key-value pairs for fast lookup - restaurant ID to Restaurant object, customer ID to Customer, order ID to Order.
keyPoints: Stores key-value pairs
Keys must be unique (duplicate key overwrites value)
Fast operations: O(1) average for get/put/remove
Initial capacity: 16, load factor: 0.75
Grows when size > capacity * load factor
No guaranteed order
Allows one null key and multiple null values
Not thread-safe (use ConcurrentHashMap)
Interview: How HashMap works? Uses hashing, stores in buckets, handles collisions with linked list/tree

concepts.name: HashSet
concepts.explanation: Unique elements only - store unique customer IDs, restaurant tags, order statuses. No duplicates allowed.
keyPoints: No duplicate elements
Backed by HashMap internally
Fast operations: O(1) average for add/remove/contains
No guaranteed order
Allows one null element
Not thread-safe
Use when uniqueness is required
Interview: HashSet vs TreeSet? HashSet faster but unordered, TreeSet sorted but slower

concepts.name: TreeSet
concepts.explanation: Sorted set - automatically sorts elements. Store restaurants by rating, orders by time, menu items alphabetically.
keyPoints: Sorted set - maintains elements in sorted order
Backed by TreeMap (Red-Black tree)
Operations: O(log n) for add/remove/contains
No duplicates
Does not allow null (throws NullPointerException)
Elements must be Comparable or provide Comparator
Use when sorted order is required
Interview: TreeSet vs HashSet? TreeSet sorted O(log n), HashSet unsorted O(1)

concepts.name: LinkedHashMap
concepts.explanation: HashMap with insertion order - track order of menu items added, maintain customer visit history.
keyPoints: Maintains insertion order (or access order)
Slightly slower than HashMap due to linked list overhead
Operations: O(1) for get/put/remove
Allows one null key and multiple null values
Useful for LRU cache implementation
Not thread-safe
Interview: HashMap vs LinkedHashMap? LinkedHashMap maintains order, HashMap doesn't

concepts.name: TreeMap
concepts.explanation: Sorted map - automatically sorts by keys. Store restaurants by rating, menu items by price, orders by timestamp.
keyPoints: Sorted map - maintains keys in sorted order
Backed by Red-Black tree
Operations: O(log n) for get/put/remove
Does not allow null keys (throws NullPointerException)
Allows null values
Keys must be Comparable or provide Comparator
Use when sorted key order is required
Interview: TreeMap vs HashMap? TreeMap sorted O(log n), HashMap unsorted O(1)

concepts.name: PriorityQueue
concepts.explanation: Queue with priority - process high-priority orders first, assign urgent deliveries, handle VIP customers.
keyPoints: Priority queue - elements ordered by priority
Backed by binary heap
Operations: O(log n) for add/poll, O(1) for peek
Default: min heap (smallest/highest priority first)
Elements must be Comparable or provide Comparator
Does not allow null
Not thread-safe
Interview: When to use PriorityQueue? When you need to process elements by priority, not insertion order

concepts.name: Stack
concepts.explanation: LIFO (Last In First Out) - undo operations, navigation history, expression evaluation.
keyPoints: LIFO: Last In First Out
Extends Vector (legacy class)
push() adds to top, pop() removes from top
peek() views top without removing
empty() checks if stack is empty
Thread-safe (synchronized methods)
Consider using Deque instead (more efficient)
Interview: Stack vs Queue? Stack LIFO, Queue FIFO

concepts.name: Queue and Deque
concepts.explanation: FIFO queue and double-ended queue - order processing queue, delivery partner assignment, task scheduling.
keyPoints: Queue: FIFO (First In First Out)
offer() adds, poll() removes, peek() views
Deque: can add/remove from both ends
ArrayDeque more efficient than LinkedList for queue/stack
ArrayDeque doesn't allow null
Not thread-safe (use ConcurrentLinkedQueue)
Interview: When to use Deque? When you need to add/remove from both ends

concepts.name: Generic Classes
concepts.explanation: Type-safe classes that work with any type - create reusable Order container, Response wrapper, Cache implementation.
keyPoints: Generics provide type safety at compile time
Eliminates need for casting
Type parameter naming: T (Type), E (Element), K (Key), V (Value)
Can have multiple type parameters: <K, V>
Type erasure: generic type info removed at runtime
Cannot create generic array: new T[10] is invalid
Interview: Why generics? Type safety, code reusability, eliminates casting

concepts.name: Generic Methods
concepts.explanation: Methods that work with different types - utility methods for printing, comparing, converting data.
keyPoints: Generic methods can be in non-generic classes
Type parameter declared before return type: <T> void method()
Bounded type parameters: <T extends Number>
Multiple bounds: <T extends Class & Interface>
Type inference: compiler determines type from arguments
Interview: Generic method vs Generic class? Method has type parameter in method signature

concepts.name: Wildcards
concepts.explanation: Flexible generic types - accept any type of list, work with unknown types, create flexible APIs.
keyPoints: ? = wildcard (unknown type)
? extends T = upper bounded (T or subclasses)
? super T = lower bounded (T or superclasses)
Use extends for reading, super for writing
PECS: Producer Extends, Consumer Super
Cannot add elements to ? extends (except null)
Interview: When to use wildcards? When you don't care about specific type

concepts.name: Lambda Expressions
concepts.explanation: Concise way to write anonymous functions - filter orders, sort restaurants, process menu items functionally.
keyPoints: Syntax: (parameters) -> expression or (parameters) -> { statements }
Parentheses optional for single parameter
Curly braces required for multiple statements
return keyword required for multiple statements
Can access final or effectively final variables
Works with functional interfaces only
Interview: What is lambda? Anonymous function that implements functional interface

concepts.name: Functional Interfaces
concepts.explanation: Interfaces with single abstract method - Predicate for filtering, Function for transformation, Consumer for processing.
keyPoints: Functional interface: interface with single abstract method (SAM)
@FunctionalInterface annotation (optional but recommended)
Predicate<T>: T → boolean (test)
Function<T,R>: T → R (apply)
Consumer<T>: T → void (accept)
Supplier<T>: () → T (get)
BiFunction<T,U,R>: (T,U) → R
Can have default and static methods
Interview: Common functional interfaces? Predicate, Function, Consumer, Supplier

concepts.name: Method References
concepts.explanation: Shorthand for lambda expressions - reference existing methods instead of writing lambda.
keyPoints: Shorthand for lambda expressions
Types: ClassName::staticMethod, object::instanceMethod, ClassName::instanceMethod, ClassName::new
More readable than lambda for simple cases
System.out::println same as x -> System.out.println(x)
String::toUpperCase same as s -> s.toUpperCase()
Order::new same as id -> new Order(id)
Interview: When to use method reference? When lambda just calls existing method

concepts.name: Stream Basics
concepts.explanation: Functional-style operations on collections - filter orders, calculate totals, transform data without loops.
keyPoints: Stream: sequence of elements supporting sequential and parallel operations
Streams are lazy - operations executed only when terminal operation called
Intermediate operations: filter, map, sorted (return Stream)
Terminal operations: collect, forEach, count (return result)
Streams can be used only once
Don't modify source during stream operations
Interview: Stream vs Collection? Stream for processing, Collection for storage

concepts.name: Stream Operations
concepts.explanation: Common stream operations - sorting, limiting, skipping, distinct, reducing data.
keyPoints: sorted() sorts in natural order, sorted(comparator) for custom
distinct() removes duplicates using equals()
limit(n) takes first n elements
skip(n) skips first n elements
min/max return Optional (may be empty)
reduce() combines elements into single result
findFirst() returns first match, findAny() for parallel streams
Interview: What is reduce? Combines stream elements using associative function

concepts.name: Collectors
concepts.explanation: Collect stream results into different forms - lists, sets, maps, grouping, partitioning.
keyPoints: Collectors.toList(), toSet(), toMap() for basic collections
groupingBy() groups elements by classifier function
partitioningBy() splits into two groups (true/false)
joining() concatenates strings
counting(), summingDouble(), averagingDouble() for aggregations
summarizingDouble() provides all statistics at once
Can combine collectors: groupingBy + counting
Interview: groupingBy vs partitioningBy? groupingBy for multiple groups, partitioningBy for two groups

concepts.name: Creating Threads
concepts.explanation: Run multiple tasks concurrently - process multiple orders simultaneously, handle multiple delivery partners, parallel restaurant updates.
keyPoints: Two ways: extend Thread or implement Runnable
Runnable preferred (allows extending other classes)
start() creates new thread and calls run()
Calling run() directly doesn't create new thread
Thread.sleep() pauses current thread
InterruptedException must be handled
Interview: Thread vs Runnable? Runnable more flexible, allows multiple inheritance

concepts.name: Thread Synchronization
concepts.explanation: Prevent race conditions when multiple threads access shared data - protect order counter, wallet balance, inventory.
keyPoints: synchronized prevents multiple threads from executing code simultaneously
synchronized method locks entire object
synchronized block locks specific object
Only one thread can execute synchronized code at a time
join() waits for thread to complete
Prevents race conditions but may cause performance overhead
Interview: What is race condition? Multiple threads accessing shared data causing inconsistent results

concepts.name: Executor Framework
concepts.explanation: Manage thread pools efficiently - handle multiple orders without creating too many threads, reuse threads.
keyPoints: ExecutorService manages thread pool
newFixedThreadPool(n): fixed number of threads
newCachedThreadPool(): creates threads as needed
newSingleThreadExecutor(): single thread
submit() submits task for execution
shutdown() stops accepting new tasks
awaitTermination() waits for tasks to complete
Interview: Why use Executor? Better thread management, reusability, control

concepts.name: Concurrent Collections
concepts.explanation: Thread-safe collections - share data between threads safely without explicit synchronization.
keyPoints: ConcurrentHashMap: thread-safe HashMap, better than synchronized HashMap
CopyOnWriteArrayList: thread-safe ArrayList, good for read-heavy scenarios
BlockingQueue: thread-safe queue with blocking operations
No need for explicit synchronization
Better performance than synchronized collections
Interview: ConcurrentHashMap vs Hashtable? ConcurrentHashMap allows concurrent reads, Hashtable locks entire map

concepts.name: Reading and Writing Files
concepts.explanation: Read/write order data, save customer information, export reports, load configuration.
keyPoints: Use try-with-resources for automatic resource closing
FileWriter/FileReader for character streams
BufferedReader/BufferedWriter for efficient reading/writing
Files class (Java 7+) provides simpler API
Paths.get() creates Path object
Files.readAllLines() reads entire file into List
Files.write() writes List to file
Interview: Why try-with-resources? Automatically closes resources, prevents leaks

concepts.name: Serialization
concepts.explanation: Convert objects to bytes for storage/transmission - save Order objects, cache Customer data, send data over network.
keyPoints: Implement Serializable interface to make class serializable
serialVersionUID for version control
transient keyword excludes field from serialization
ObjectOutputStream for serialization
ObjectInputStream for deserialization
All fields must be Serializable
static fields not serialized
Interview: What is serialization? Converting object to byte stream for storage/transmission

concepts.name: static Keyword
concepts.explanation: Belongs to class, not instance - shared counter for all orders, utility methods, constants.
keyPoints: static variable: shared by all instances
static method: called on class, not object
static block: initialization code, runs once
Cannot access instance members from static context
Can access static members from instance context
Interview: When to use static? Utility methods, constants, shared data

concepts.name: final Keyword

concepts.name: All Access Modifiers & Non-Access Modifiers
concepts.explanation: Complete modifier reference — what each keyword means and where it can be applied. Know these cold for code design and interviews.
concepts.code:
// ACCESS MODIFIERS
public    → accessible everywhere
protected → same package + subclasses
(default) → same package only
private   → same class only

// NON-ACCESS MODIFIERS
static    → belongs to class, not instance
final     → can't reassign / override / subclass
abstract  → must be implemented by subclass
synchronized → one thread at a time
volatile  → always read from main memory
transient → skip during serialization
native    → implemented in native code (C/C++)
strictfp  → strict IEEE 754 float precision

// WHERE THEY APPLY
Class   : public, abstract, final, strictfp
Field   : all access + static, final, volatile, transient
Method  : all access + static, final, abstract, synchronized, native
Local   : final only
keyPoints: A class can be public or package-private (default) only — never private or protected
final field: must be initialized in declaration or constructor
static final = constant (UPPER_SNAKE_CASE naming)
abstract method has no body; class with abstract method must be abstract
volatile prevents caching of variable in CPU registers — ensures visibility
transient skips field during ObjectOutputStream serialization
synchronized method locks on 'this'; synchronized static method locks on Class object
Interview: Can abstract class have constructor? Yes — called via super() from child

concepts.name: All Java Keywords Quick Reference
concepts.explanation: Every Java reserved keyword categorized by purpose. These cannot be used as identifiers.
keyPoints: Java has 52 reserved keywords — none can be used as variable/method/class names
goto and const are reserved but not used
var (Java 10+): local variable type inference — not a keyword, context-sensitive
yield (Java 14+): returns value from switch expression
record (Java 16+): concise immutable data class
sealed (Java 17+): restricts which classes can extend/implement
permits used with sealed: lists allowed subclasses
when (Java 21+): guard clause in pattern matching switch

concepts.name: Type System Quick Reference
concepts.explanation: Primitive types, their sizes, ranges, and wrapper classes. Autoboxing/unboxing reference. Default values for fields.
keyPoints: int is the default integer type; double is the default decimal type
Integer cache: -128 to 127 are cached — use .equals() not == for Integer comparison
float has ~7 significant digits; double has ~15 — always prefer double for precision
char is an unsigned 16-bit integer; can do char arithmetic
Autoboxing has overhead — avoid in tight loops (prefer primitives for performance)
null is valid for any reference type but causes NullPointerException when dereferenced
Interview: What is Integer cache? JVM caches Integer -128 to 127 for memory efficiency
Widening order: byte → short → int → long → float → double

concepts.name: Exception Hierarchy Quick Reference
concepts.explanation: The complete Java exception hierarchy — know what to catch, what to declare, and what you can't recover from.
concepts.code:
Throwable
├── Error (don't catch these!)
│   ├── OutOfMemoryError
│   ├── StackOverflowError
│   ├── AssertionError
│   └── VirtualMachineError
│
└── Exception
    ├── CHECKED (must catch or declare 'throws')
    │   ├── IOException
    │   │   ├── FileNotFoundException
    │   │   └── EOFException
    │   ├── SQLException
    │   ├── ClassNotFoundException
    │   └── InterruptedException
    │
    └── RuntimeException (UNCHECKED — optional catch)
        ├── NullPointerException
        ├── ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
        ├── ClassCastException
        ├── IllegalArgumentException
        ├── IllegalStateException
        ├── ArithmeticException
        ├── NumberFormatException
        ├── UnsupportedOperationException
        └── ConcurrentModificationException
keyPoints: Checked exceptions: compiler forces you to handle (try-catch) or declare (throws)
Unchecked (RuntimeException): optional to catch, usually indicate programming bugs
Errors: JVM-level problems — should NOT be caught in most cases
Multi-catch: catch (IOException | SQLException e) — clean, since Java 7
try-with-resources: auto-closes AutoCloseable — since Java 7
Exception chaining: throw new RuntimeException("msg", originalException)
Finally always runs except: System.exit(), JVM crash, or infinite loop
Interview: Difference between Error and Exception? Error = JVM problem (unrecoverable)

concepts.name: Collections Complexity Cheat Sheet
concepts.explanation: Time complexity of all major collection operations at a glance. Essential for DSA interviews and choosing the right data structure.
keyPoints: ArrayList: best for indexed access; LinkedList: best for frequent add/remove at head/tail
HashSet/HashMap: O(1) average but O(n) worst case (hash collision)
TreeSet/TreeMap: always O(log n) — backed by Red-Black tree (self-balancing BST)
PriorityQueue is a min-heap — smallest element at head (use Comparator for max-heap)
ArrayDeque is the preferred Stack and Queue implementation (faster than Stack/LinkedList)
ConcurrentHashMap: thread-safe HashMap with segment locking — O(1) average
Collections.unmodifiableList() wraps list — throws UnsupportedOperationException on write
Interview: Which Map maintains insertion order? LinkedHashMap

concepts.name: String — All Essential Methods
concepts.explanation: Every important String method with signature and example. Strings are immutable — all methods return a new String.
concepts.code:
String s = "  Hello, Java World!  ";

// LENGTH & CHARS
s.length()                    // 22
s.charAt(2)                   // 'H'
s.indexOf('J')                // 8
s.lastIndexOf('o')            // 15
s.isEmpty()                   // false
s.isBlank()                   // false (Java 11+)

// COMPARISON
s.equals("other")             // false
s.equalsIgnoreCase("HELLO")   // false
s.compareTo("another")        // +/- int
s.startsWith("  Hello")       // true
s.endsWith("!  ")             // true
s.contains("Java")            // true

// EXTRACTION
s.substring(2)                // "Hello, Java World!  "
s.substring(2, 7)             // "Hello"
s.trim()                      // "Hello, Java World!"
s.strip()                     // "Hello, Java World!" (Unicode-aware)
s.stripLeading()              // Java 11+
s.stripTrailing()             // Java 11+

// TRANSFORM
s.toUpperCase()               // "  HELLO, JAVA WORLD!  "
s.toLowerCase()               // "  hello, java world!  "
s.replace('l', 'L')           // replaces all 'l'
s.replace("Java", "Python")   // replaces first match
s.replaceAll("[aeiou]", "*") // regex replace
s.replaceFirst("\\s+", "-")  // regex, first only

// SPLIT & JOIN
s.split(",")                  // ["  Hello", " Java World!  "]
s.split(",", 2)               // limit splits
String.join("-", "a","b","c") // "a-b-c"
String.join(",", list)        // join from collection

// FORMAT
String.format("Name: %s Age: %d", "Raj", 25)
formatted = "Price: %.2f".formatted(99.5); // Java 15+

// CHECK
Character.isDigit('5')        // true
Character.isLetter('A')       // true
Character.isWhitespace(' ')   // true
Character.toUpperCase('a')    // 'A'

// CONVERT
String.valueOf(42)            // "42"
Integer.parseInt("42")        // 42
Double.parseDouble("3.14")    // 3.14
"hello".toCharArray()         // char[]

// REPEAT & LINES (Java 11+)
"ab".repeat(3)                // "ababab"
"line1\nline2".lines()        // Stream<String>
"  ".isBlank()                // true
keyPoints: All String methods return NEW String — original is unchanged (immutable)
indexOf returns -1 if not found
substring(start, end): start is inclusive, end is exclusive
trim() removes ASCII whitespace; strip() removes Unicode whitespace (Java 11+)
split() uses regex — escape special chars: split("\\.")
String.format() format specifiers: %s=String, %d=int, %f=float, %n=newline, %b=boolean
compareTo: returns 0 if equal, negative if less, positive if greater (lexicographic)
Interview: Why is String immutable? Security, string pool, thread-safety, hashcode caching

concepts.name: StringBuilder & StringBuffer — Methods
concepts.explanation: Mutable string builders. StringBuilder is non-thread-safe (faster); StringBuffer is thread-safe (slower). Use StringBuilder unless shared across threads.
keyPoints: StringBuilder is ~3-5x faster than String concatenation in loops — use it!
Initial capacity is 16; doubles when full (amortized O(1) append)
Chaining: sb.append("a").append("b").reverse() — all methods return 'this'
StringBuffer has same API but all methods are synchronized (thread-safe)
delete(start, end): end is exclusive (same as substring)
sb.toString() is required to get the final String value
Interview: String vs StringBuilder vs StringBuffer? String immutable, SB mutable fast, StringBuffer mutable thread-safe
Compiler converts String + String + String into StringBuilder internally (except in loops)

concepts.name: Math Class — All Essential Methods
concepts.explanation: java.lang.Math is a utility class with static methods for mathematical operations. Automatically imported.
concepts.code:
// BASIC
Math.abs(-5)          // 5      (absolute value)
Math.max(3, 7)        // 7
Math.min(3, 7)        // 3
Math.signum(-4.0)     // -1.0   (sign of number)

// ROUNDING
Math.ceil(3.2)        // 4.0    (round up)
Math.floor(3.9)       // 3.0    (round down)
Math.round(3.5)       // 4      (round half-up)
Math.rint(3.5)        // 4.0    (round to nearest even)

// POWER & ROOTS
Math.pow(2, 10)       // 1024.0 (2^10)
Math.sqrt(16)         // 4.0
Math.cbrt(27)         // 3.0    (cube root)
Math.hypot(3, 4)      // 5.0    (√(x²+y²))

// LOGARITHM & EXP
Math.log(Math.E)      // 1.0    (natural log)
Math.log10(1000)      // 3.0
Math.exp(1)           // 2.718... (e^x)

// TRIG (radians)
Math.sin(Math.PI/2)   // 1.0
Math.cos(0)           // 1.0
Math.tan(Math.PI/4)   // 1.0
Math.toRadians(180)   // Math.PI
Math.toDegrees(Math.PI) // 180.0

// CONSTANTS
Math.PI               // 3.141592653589793
Math.E                // 2.718281828459045
Integer.MAX_VALUE     // 2147483647
Integer.MIN_VALUE     // -2147483648
Long.MAX_VALUE        // 9223372036854775807
Double.MAX_VALUE      // ~1.8e308
Double.MIN_VALUE      // ~4.9e-324 (smallest positive)
Double.POSITIVE_INFINITY
Double.NEGATIVE_INFINITY
Double.NaN            // Not a Number

// RANDOM (0.0 inclusive to 1.0 exclusive)
Math.random()         // random double
// Better: use java.util.Random
Random rng = new Random();
rng.nextInt(100)      // 0 to 99
rng.nextDouble()      // 0.0 to 1.0
rng.nextBoolean()
rng.nextLong()
keyPoints: All Math methods are static — call as Math.abs(), never create Math object
Math.round(x): returns long for double, int for float — result is (long)(Math.floor(x+0.5))
Math.random() returns [0.0, 1.0) — multiply and cast: (int)(Math.random() * n) for 0..n-1
NaN: Double.isNaN(x) to check — NaN != NaN is TRUE (weird but correct)
Infinity: 1.0/0.0 = POSITIVE_INFINITY, -1.0/0.0 = NEGATIVE_INFINITY (no exception for double)
Integer division by zero throws ArithmeticException; double division does NOT
Interview: Math.round(2.5) vs Math.round(-2.5)? Returns 3 and -2 (half-up rounding)

concepts.name: Arrays Class — All Essential Methods
concepts.explanation: java.util.Arrays provides utility methods for array manipulation. All methods are static.
keyPoints: Arrays.sort uses Dual-Pivot Quicksort for primitives (O(n log n) average)
Arrays.sort for Objects uses TimSort (stable, O(n log n))
binarySearch REQUIRES a sorted array — undefined behavior on unsorted
Arrays.asList() returns a fixed-size list (add/remove throws UnsupportedOperationException)
For a fully mutable list: new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList(arr))
Arrays.copyOfRange(arr, from, to): from is inclusive, to is exclusive
Arrays.parallelSort(): uses ForkJoinPool — faster for large arrays (>8192 elements)
Interview: How to convert int[] to List? Use IntStream: IntStream.of(a).boxed().collect(Collectors.toList())

concepts.name: Collections Class — All Essential Methods
concepts.explanation: java.util.Collections provides utility algorithms for Collection objects (not arrays). All methods are static.
keyPoints: Collections.sort() is stable (preserves relative order of equal elements)
unmodifiable wrappers throw UnsupportedOperationException on write — not truly immutable
For truly immutable: List.of(), Set.of(), Map.of() (Java 9+) — also null-unsafe
Collections.copy(dest, src): dest list must already have >= src.size() elements
frequency() is O(n) — iterates through entire collection
disjoint() returns true if the two collections have NO elements in common
synchronizedList iterates require manual synchronized block: synchronized(list){ for(...){} }
Interview: Collections vs Collection? Collections = utility class; Collection = root interface

concepts.name: List, Map, Set — Creation & Essential Operations
concepts.explanation: Modern Java (9+) factory methods and all core operations for List, Map, and Set interfaces.
keyPoints: List.of(), Set.of(), Map.of() (Java 9+): immutable, null-disallowed, unmodifiable
Map.of() is unordered; Map.ofEntries() for more than 10 entries
map.getOrDefault() avoids null check: map.getOrDefault(key, fallback)
computeIfAbsent: creates value only if key is missing — great for grouping
merge(key, value, remapping): if key absent → puts value; else → applies function to old+new
entrySet().stream() is the most flexible way to process Map entries
TreeSet/TreeMap: elements must implement Comparable OR provide Comparator
Interview: How to iterate a Map? for(Map.Entry e : map.entrySet()) or map.forEach()

concepts.name: Stream API — All Essential Operations
concepts.explanation: Java 8+ Stream API for functional-style data processing. Streams are lazy, one-shot, and cannot be reused.
concepts.code:
import java.util.stream.*;

List<String> items = List.of("Biryani","Pizza","Burger","Pasta","Idli");

// CREATE STREAMS
items.stream()                         // from Collection
Arrays.stream(arr)                     // from array
Stream.of("a", "b", "c")              // from values
Stream.iterate(0, n -> n + 2)          // infinite: 0,2,4...
Stream.generate(Math::random)           // infinite supplier
IntStream.range(0, 5)                  // 0,1,2,3,4
IntStream.rangeClosed(1, 5)            // 1,2,3,4,5

// INTERMEDIATE (lazy — return Stream)
.filter(s -> s.length() > 4)          // keep matching
.map(s -> s.toUpperCase())            // transform
.mapToInt(String::length)             // Stream→IntStream
.flatMap(s -> Stream.of(s.split(""))  // flatten
.distinct()                           // unique values
.sorted()                             // natural order
.sorted(Comparator.reverseOrder())    // custom order
.sorted(Comparator.comparing(String::length))
.peek(s -> System.out.println(s))     // debug (no change)
.limit(3)                             // take first 3
.skip(2)                              // skip first 2
.takeWhile(s -> s.length() < 6)       // Java 9+
.dropWhile(s -> s.length() < 6)       // Java 9+

// TERMINAL (eager — triggers execution)
.collect(Collectors.toList())         // to List
.collect(Collectors.toSet())          // to Set
.collect(Collectors.joining(", "))    // "a, b, c"
.collect(Collectors.joining(",","[","]")) // "[a,b,c]"
.collect(Collectors.counting())       // count
.collect(Collectors.groupingBy(String::length))  // Map<Int,List>
.collect(Collectors.partitioningBy(s -> s.length()>4)) // Map<Bool,List>
.collect(Collectors.toMap(s->s, String::length)) // to Map
.collect(Collectors.summingInt(String::length))
.collect(Collectors.averagingInt(String::length))
.collect(Collectors.summarizingInt(String::length)) // stats
.forEach(System.out::println)         // iterate (terminal)
.count()                              // long
.findFirst()                          // Optional<T>
.findAny()                            // Optional<T> (parallel)
.anyMatch(s -> s.startsWith("B"))    // boolean
.allMatch(s -> s.length() > 2)        // boolean
.noneMatch(s -> s.isEmpty())          // boolean
.min(Comparator.naturalOrder())       // Optional<T>
.max(Comparator.naturalOrder())       // Optional<T>
.reduce(0, Integer::sum)              // fold to single value
.toArray()                            // Object[]

// NUMERIC STREAMS
IntStream.of(1,2,3).sum()             // 6
IntStream.of(1,2,3).average()         // OptionalDouble
IntStream.of(1,2,3).min()             // OptionalInt
IntStream.of(1,2,3).max()             // OptionalInt
IntStream.of(1,2,3).boxed()           // Stream<Integer>
keyPoints: Streams are LAZY: intermediate ops don't run until a terminal op is called
Streams are ONE-SHOT: cannot be reused after a terminal op — create new stream
map() transforms each element; flatMap() transforms and flattens one level
Collectors.groupingBy() returns Map<K, List<V>> — perfect for grouping
reduce(identity, accumulator): identity is the starting value (0 for sum, 1 for product)
Parallel streams: list.parallelStream() — uses ForkJoinPool, not always faster
Optional: check .isPresent() or use .orElse(default) before calling .get()
Interview: Intermediate vs Terminal operations? Intermediate return Stream (lazy); Terminal return result (eager)

concepts.name: Optional — Methods & Patterns
concepts.explanation: Java 8+ Optional<T> wraps a potentially-null value. Eliminates explicit null checks and NullPointerExceptions when used properly.
keyPoints: Never use Optional.get() without isPresent() — prefer orElse/orElseGet/orElseThrow
Optional.of() throws NullPointerException if value is null — use ofNullable for uncertain values
map() vs flatMap(): use flatMap when the mapping function itself returns Optional
Don't use Optional as a field type or method parameter — only as return type
orElseGet() is lazy (uses Supplier) — prefer over orElse for expensive defaults
Optional.stream() enables using optionals in stream pipelines (Java 9+)
filter() returns Optional.empty() if predicate is false, otherwise same Optional
Interview: When to use Optional? As return type of methods that might not return a value

concepts.name: Lambda & Functional Interfaces — Quick Reference
concepts.explanation: Functional interface signatures, lambda syntax, and method references. Core of Java's functional programming style.
keyPoints: @FunctionalInterface: exactly one abstract method — enables lambda assignment
Lambdas can capture effectively final variables from enclosing scope
Method reference is shorthand for lambda when lambda just calls a method
Predicate.not(Predicate) (Java 11+): Predicate.not(String::isBlank)
Consumer.andThen(after): chain consumers — c1.andThen(c2) runs c1 then c2
BiFunction<T,U,R>: most generic — takes two inputs, returns one output
Primitive functional interfaces (IntFunction, etc.) avoid autoboxing overhead
Interview: What makes an interface functional? Exactly one abstract method (can have default/static methods)

concepts.name: Comparable & Comparator — Sorting Reference
concepts.explanation: Two ways to define ordering in Java. Comparable is for natural order; Comparator is for custom/external ordering.
keyPoints: Comparable: natural order (one fixed order per class) — used by sort/TreeSet/TreeMap by default
Comparator: external order (multiple possible orders) — passed to sort/TreeSet/TreeMap
Comparator.comparing() takes a key extractor (Function) — cleanest way to sort by field
thenComparing(): secondary sort when primary values are equal
NEVER use subtraction (a - b) for int comparison — use Integer.compare(a, b) to avoid overflow
Comparator.reversed() inverts the ordering without rewriting logic
TreeMap/TreeSet store entries in compareTo/compare order — maintains sorted structure
Interview: Comparable vs Comparator? Comparable = class defines own order; Comparator = external ordering

concepts.name: Generics — Syntax & Wildcards
concepts.explanation: Generics provide type safety without casting. Wildcards (? extends T, ? super T) enable flexible APIs.
keyPoints: Type erasure: generic type info removed at runtime — only exists at compile time
Cannot create generic arrays: new T[10] is illegal
Cannot use primitives as type params: List<int> is illegal — use List<Integer>
PECS: Producer Extends (read from), Consumer Super (write to)
? extends T: you can read as T, but cannot add (except null)
? super T: you can add T (or subtype), but reads return Object
Unbounded wildcard List<?>: use when you don't care about type at all
Interview: What is type erasure? JVM removes generic type info at runtime for backward compatibility

concepts.name: Multithreading — Essential Syntax & Methods
concepts.explanation: Core threading APIs: Thread creation, Runnable, synchronization, and java.util.concurrent essentials.
keyPoints: ALWAYS call thread.start() — calling run() directly executes in current thread, not new thread
Prefer ExecutorService over raw Thread — manages thread pools and lifecycle
synchronized method locks on 'this'; synchronized static method locks on Class object
Thread.sleep() throws checked InterruptedException — must handle
join() makes calling thread wait until the target thread completes
volatile ensures visibility across threads but NOT atomicity
AtomicInteger, AtomicLong, AtomicReference for lock-free thread-safe operations
Interview: Difference between synchronized and volatile? synchronized ensures atomicity + visibility; volatile only visibility

concepts.name: File I/O — Modern NIO.2 Quick Reference
concepts.explanation: Modern Java file operations using java.nio.file.Files and Path API (Java 7+). Prefer these over legacy File class.
keyPoints: Files.readString()/writeString() (Java 11+): simplest way to read/write text files
Files.lines(): returns lazy Stream — must close with try-with-resources
Path.of() (Java 11+) vs Paths.get() (Java 7+): prefer Path.of()
Always use try-with-resources for streams and I/O — ensures close() is called
StandardOpenOption: APPEND, CREATE, CREATE_NEW, TRUNCATE_EXISTING, READ, WRITE
Files.walk() is recursive — call .close() or use try-with-resources to avoid resource leak
Prefer Files.deleteIfExists() over Files.delete() to avoid NoSuchFileException
Interview: NIO.2 vs legacy File? Files/Path has better error handling, symlink support, and is more complete

concepts.name: Date & Time API — java.time Quick Reference
concepts.explanation: Java 8+ Date-Time API. Immutable and thread-safe. Replaces java.util.Date and Calendar entirely.
keyPoints: ALL java.time classes are IMMUTABLE — plus/minus/with always return new instance
LocalDate, LocalTime, LocalDateTime have no timezone — use ZonedDateTime for timezone-aware
Instant is for machine timestamps (microseconds since epoch) — use for logging/DB timestamps
Duration.between(start, end) for time gaps; Period.between(startDate, endDate) for date gaps
DateTimeFormatter.ISO_LOCAL_DATE = yyyy-MM-dd; ISO_LOCAL_DATE_TIME = yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss
Never use java.util.Date or Calendar in new code — use java.time instead
ChronoUnit.DAYS.between(date1, date2): convenient for computing difference in specific units
Interview: Why new Date-Time API? Old Date/Calendar was mutable, poorly designed, and not thread-safe

concepts.name: Integer, Long, Double — Wrapper Class Methods
concepts.explanation: Essential static and instance methods of the numeric wrapper classes for parsing, converting, and operating on numbers.
concepts.code:
// INTEGER
Integer.parseInt("42")          // int: parse String
Integer.parseInt("FF", 16)      // int: parse hex → 255
Integer.valueOf(42)             // Integer: boxing
Integer.toString(42)            // "42"
Integer.toBinaryString(10)      // "1010"
Integer.toHexString(255)        // "ff"
Integer.toOctalString(8)        // "10"
Integer.MAX_VALUE               // 2147483647
Integer.MIN_VALUE               // -2147483648
Integer.compare(a, b)           // -1, 0, or 1
Integer.max(a, b)               // larger of two
Integer.min(a, b)               // smaller
Integer.sum(a, b)               // a + b (for method refs)
Integer.bitCount(7)             // 3 (number of 1-bits)
Integer.reverse(n)              // reverse bits
Integer.highestOneBit(n)        // highest set bit
Integer.numberOfLeadingZeros(n)
Integer.numberOfTrailingZeros(n)

// LONG
Long.parseLong("12345678900")   // long
Long.MAX_VALUE                  // 9223372036854775807
Long.compare(a, b)
Long.toBinaryString(100L)

// DOUBLE
Double.parseDouble("3.14")      // double
Double.isNaN(x)                 // true if NaN
Double.isInfinite(x)            // true if ±Infinity
Double.compare(a, b)            // -1, 0, 1 (handles NaN safely)
Double.MAX_VALUE
Double.MIN_VALUE                // smallest positive double (~4.9e-324)

// CHARACTER
Character.isDigit('5')          // true
Character.isLetter('A')         // true
Character.isLetterOrDigit('3')  // true
Character.isWhitespace(' ')     // true
Character.isUpperCase('A')      // true
Character.isLowerCase('a')      // true
Character.toUpperCase('a')      // 'A'
Character.toLowerCase('A')      // 'a'
Character.getNumericValue('5')  // 5
Character.getNumericValue('A')  // 10 (hex)

// BOOLEAN
Boolean.parseBoolean("true")    // true
Boolean.toString(true)          // "true"
keyPoints: Integer.parseInt() vs Integer.valueOf(): parseInt returns int (primitive); valueOf returns Integer (object)
Always use Integer.compare(a, b) for int comparison in Comparator — avoids subtraction overflow
Integer cache: -128 to 127 cached — Integer.valueOf(127) == Integer.valueOf(127) is TRUE
Double.compare() handles NaN correctly; direct == and < do not
Character methods work on char primitives — very useful in string processing/DSA
Integer.bitCount(n): counts set bits — commonly used in bit manipulation problems
toBinaryString/toHexString: no sign extension for negative values (uses unsigned representation)
Interview: Integer.parseInt vs Integer.valueOf? parseInt returns primitive, valueOf returns Integer object
